title: Colleges you need to avoid...
body: I'm 26 years old and will be attending a private university in Austin, Texas this fall semester! (I'm really excited after returning back to my home city from new student orientation). I avoided college until mainly because I lacked the structure from not understanding what I'd like to do as a career. Honestly I wasn't aware of the way private universities work (or at least the school where I'm a student at). I just avoided private schools due to the cost. It blows my mind about how opposite my personal case worked out! Summary: 50,000 a year. I received federal grants, 3 school grants, then applied for the 2 small loans my fafsa could offer. My total per semester as a full time on campus student with meal plan is five thousand a semester. That's it. I can more than work with only 10K a year if it means I get to be a part of that campus I've already found my belonging with. My lack of research could have made this happen years earlier ago for me, but once admissions got into contact with me I've gained many big new insights. That is how I found this video because I'm so interesting in learning more about this new concept in my life lol
author: @BrookePainter

title: Colleges you need to avoid...

title: Colleges you need to avoid...
body: My daughter applied to 6 or 7 colleges and universities.   I assumed the state schools would be cheaper. I was incorrect.   The private college gave my daughter a competitive scholarship based on her top 5% achievements in high school.   Still insanely expensive but the college has a solid internship program which I believe is very important.
author: @jjs777fzr

title: Colleges you need to avoid...
body: Correction to the private vs public schools point: private colleges often offer generous financial aid that can make it cheaper to attend than a state school. State schools offer offer almost no institutional aid since they don't have the large endowments of private and are already offering such a reduced tuition to begin with. If you are interested in a non profit accredited private college then go ahead and apply and see how much fin aid they offer you and what the conditions to receive it are. Don't write off private colleges as "too expensive" just by looking at the sticker price of cost of attendance because almost nobody besides the super rich actually pay that amount.
author: @first9299

title: Colleges you need to avoid...
body:
Ok this is the way I am doing it
1. I took many AP and Dual credit classes during my High School time
2. I went to my local community college for a Summer and 2 semesters.
3. I transferred to a University less than an hour away from my parents house, so I could stay with them.
I am currently in my penultimate semester, expected to graduate in May of 2024, and so far I have only spent $14,824 in tuition (I just need to pay 4,275 for my last semester and that’s it)

body: Accreditation by the Dept. of Education is not valuable. Make sure it is accredited by a REGIONAL accreditation agency. It seems counterproductive, but national accreditation agencies have less recognition than regional ones.

title: my experience at stony brook university
body: Ayeee cool vid! Would definitely love to see some more stony brook vids as it’s probably my top choice in terms of unis. If you want am idea consider something like a stats video or when you’re on campus again perhaps do a 1 week food tour (Brownie in stony did one but kinda just want more info). I’m quite concerned regarding my academics, my gpa in school was absolutely trash as I simply didn’t try to perform well (I’m actually studying outside usa so its a bit different here). Basically we’d focus on end of year grades and performance on board exams. In short, I moved from new york to Bangladesh and I study under UK curriculum so it’s a bit difficult to explain. But basically I had 3 A*, 1 B, and 1 C in o levels (basically final year exam for 10th grade) and for A levels (11th grade final year exam) I had 3 A’s, 4 B’s, 1 C, and a D. Here these grades are considered quite good (to give you an idea universities like UAlbany, UB grant AP credits to any subject you get C or higher in A levels as it’s integrated into our curricula) but not quite sure if it would hold it’s weight at stony brook or at other unis in general. Im planning to move back to new york for uni but at the same time don’t want to be far outside my comfort zone of NYC. As per my extracurriculars they are probably my strongest point as I’m the founder and executive adviser of a foundation which has helped thousands of underprivileged people in Bangladesh with weeks of food supplies and medicine. P.S dropped a sub and push notifications, hoping for some more stony vids perhaps even story times! Rock on 🤘
author: @TasfiqurRob

title: College Advice (kind of) || What Stony Brook University is really like
body: Having graduated from Stony Brook, I can relate on some of your experiences. From reflecting my own journey I would suggest load up on as many easy dec courses as possible  your first year to get used to college to boost your gpa. And take math requirements at Suffolk  community college and have your math and science requirements transferred over that way you bi pass those and they do not hurt your gpa.
author: @64wulf45

title: College Advice (kind of) || What Stony Brook University is really like
body: I would like to point out something for freshman looking to major in Computer Science. Personally my high school didn't offer any computer science courses whatsoever so I should be able to reach out to those with 0 programming experience. From my experience, I can say that to get through the prereqs you have to be passionate about CS. By that i mean stay committed to learning and going out of your way to make sure you effectively learn the material. You will find people who may come off as the next Bill Gates and be programming geniuses but trust me, the only thing differentiating you and them is practice. Ive been there and honestly, just work on your pace, don't be intimidated whatsoever. Make your own programs to get used to coding. They dont have to be hardcore, start off with something simple like a basic calculator. The more you practice, the more hooked you will be to the subject and the better you will get. I didnt really have any programming experience as I mentioned but I did spend the summer before my freshman year learning how to code. There are plenty of tutorials at your disposal, use them and apply them in your own ways. I do agree with what she said that you have to be hard working, but also leave some space where you can put your creativity to use. CS is something you can make your own through coding so let your ideas flourish. Of course make sure you prioritize classes first but in your free time keep practicing, it will make you confident in your abilities.

title: Before YOU Attend STONY BROOK Just Hear Me Out !
body: Broo don’t go to school yet if you don’t know what you want and go back later!! Trust me it’s completely normal to have no clue what you want to do. Explore until you figure it out. I just graduated from Oneonta as a Communications major and I was exactly like you, but now I’m back in undergrad studying medicine and I absolutely love every second of it. If I originally waited until I found what I wanted I wouldn’t have wasted that time, money and grades! Drop out now and do what you want until you figure it out!!

title: A day in the life of an electrical engineering student
body:
I was an aerospace engineer (BS, MS AE) for 40 years, recently retired. I understand exactly how you feel, an engineering degree takes ALOT of mental stamina, more than most non engineers realize, I can remember wanting to quit many times. My advice to you is DON’T QUIT!! Engineering is a GREAT career field, right now a whole generation of baby boomer engineers are retiring and the opportunities are tremendous (especially for an electrical engineer). Take a SHORT break if you need too, but then get back up on the horse, you won’t be sorry!! Much RESPECT!!
😊❤
author: @boxorfurnace

title: A day in the life of an electrical engineering student
body:
I remember my engineering student days. I am now a graduate of Electrical Engineering, now reviewing for My REE Board Exam this August, please pray for me! God bless you all! ❤️

We can do this! We will be successful licensed engineers!

author: @lunnaMII

title: A day in the life of an electrical engineering student
body: The Title says "Electrical" but they have a picture of a pcb with what looks like a microprocessor hidden behind the little patch of words and that's not Electrical, it is Electronics.  I have been a Power Electronics Engineer for the past 50 or more years.  I love it.  I enjoyed every day I went to work.  Yeah, classes were hard and we really had to make it our lifes work.  BUT... 4 years go quickly.  There was a time that each day on my way to work, I thought, "what is todays adventure going to be".  I cant get there fast enough to enjoy myself.  So Rachel Brownell put your heart into it and you will succeed. The rewards waiting for you are tremendous.
author: @ferrariguy4396

author: @jaidynparsell

title: NURSING SCHOOL WEEK IN THE LIFE | a rough week, mental breakdown, 3 clinicals, exam
body: I had a legit mental breakdown just during my prereqs and studying for the HESI! I was doing nothing but studying every day for weeks and weeks on end, and eventually I just lost it. It took me about two weeks of daily meditation and mindfulness to get back to myself. Nursing is no joke!
author: @periiwinkl

title: NURSING SCHOOL WEEK IN THE LIFE | a rough week, mental breakdown, 3 clinicals, exam
body: U got this!! I was in ur same spot last year and now am working and it is worth it all! Keep the lord close and it will all work out!
author: @itselliehern1010

title: NURSING SCHOOL WEEK IN THE LIFE | a rough week, mental breakdown, 3 clinicals, exam
body: Girl tell me about it, I started college in summer of 23 after I graduated high school and I took my CNA class cause we have to be a CNA to be admitted to the program, taking 5 classes in the fall for gen eds, then more in the spring and more over summer, then i got accepted into the nursing program so I was doing nursing and two accelerated classes and now im in my 2/4 semester of nursing school taking 2 more gen eds. It's rough. I'm getting my ADN and it's a 2-year program and it's quite a lot. But, we will do great :D
author: @gabrielhelm5685

title: NURSING SCHOOL WEEK IN THE LIFE | a rough week, mental breakdown, 3 clinicals, exam

title: NURSING SCHOOL WEEK IN THE LIFE | a rough week, mental breakdown, 3 clinicals, exam
body: I went to nursing school back in 2022 & the school I went to towards the middle/end we had exams mondays & Fridays and clinicals Tuesday, Wednesday & Thursdays every week. It was rough for a while. You got this! You’ll be done before you know it and then you’ll be like I will never do that again.
author: @patricialeann

title: NURSING SCHOOL WEEK IN THE LIFE | a rough week, mental breakdown, 3 clinicals, exam
body: I’ve never watched something as relatable in my life lol I just starte day BSN program and planned on working through it as a CNA. Had to learn real quick to seriously cut back hours and just prioritize school and SLEEP. I still work 25 hrs a week and it’s rough but we will get there eventually. Working hard always pays off in the future

title: NURSING SCHOOL WEEK IN THE LIFE | a rough week, mental breakdown, 3 clinicals, exam
body:
I’ve been a nurse for almost 10 years now… but I remember the struggle of just being overloaded with exams every week. They probably do this to help get you prepared for your nursing career.
When I was a new nurse, I was exhausted. You need to figure out time management, working your “3” days a week and then coming home exhausted and going back again.
It does get better!
author: @paigebear4788

title: Stony Brook University Campus Tour
body: stonybrook is a nursing school. Biology is the key to this school. Microbiology trash because tedinitis we can't drink liquor as drug because it is going burn us salmonella, is ecoli even a word? Doing this break all the bones in our body. we have insulin pumps vomit which without liquor break bones. Biochemistry now we have chemotherapy anesthesia murder patients? Nurses are going to make drugs now? Stupid, physiology neurology bullshit so we have chiropractors who break our bones? Nerve cords are in reality bone marrows. So we have chiropractors who squeeze out our bone marrows? Winners would be paleontology looking at our bones for physical therapy, cellular biology our stem cell skin cells, organic chemistry which wasn't offered in our school looking at liquors and names, regular chemistry looking at the drugs, also psychology looking at our hearts therapist. These are the courses nurses should know so we get the real deals. Don't mix chemisty with biology. One is drugs one is hospital. At the most mix physics with chemistry not biology.
author: @beiyangshi9821

You can't be afraid of taking drugs if you want to work in a pharmacy.
